meta.title: 1.3 Links, Images, and Lists
meta.description: Read and troubleshoot the most common HTML elements: links, images, and lists, including their attributes and common breakages.

blocks.prompt: You click a link on a webpage and nothing happens. Or an image shows a broken icon instead of a photo. What do you think causes those problems in the HTML?

blocks.content:
## Beyond headings and paragraphs So far you have seen tags that label text: headings, paragraphs, sections. But webpages are interactive and visual — they have links to click, images to display, and lists of items. These elements introduce something new: **attributes**. ## Attributes: extra information on a tag An attribute is additional information attached to an opening tag. It follows a pattern: `name="value"`. ```html Click here ``` In this example: - `` is the tag (short for "anchor" — it creates a link) - `href="https://example.com"` is an attribute that tells the browser *where* the link goes - "Click here" is the visible text the user sees and clicks The tag says *what* it is (a link). The attribute says *how* it behaves (where it goes). ## Links: the `` tag Links are everywhere on the web, and they break in predictable ways: ```html See our pricing See our pricing See our pricing ``` If a link looks clickable but does nothing, check the `href` attribute. It is either missing, empty, or pointing to a wrong URL. This is one of the most common issues you will encounter. ## Images: the `` tag Images work differently from most tags — they are **self-closing**, meaning there is no separate closing tag: ```html ``` - `src` (source) — The file path or URL of the image. If this is wrong, the image will not load. - `alt` (alternative text) — A description that appears if the image cannot load, and that screen readers read aloud to visually impaired users. ### Common image problems **Broken image (wrong path):** ```html ``` You will see a broken image icon. The fix is correcting the `src` path. **Missing alt text:** ```html ``` The image displays fine, but screen readers have nothing to announce, and if the image ever fails to load, users see no description. Always check for `alt` attributes — their absence is an accessibility problem. ## Lists: `
    `, `
      `, and `
    1. ` HTML has two kinds of lists: **Unordered list** (bullet points): ```html
      • Feature one
      • Feature two
      • Feature three
      ``` **Ordered list** (numbered): ```html
      1. Preheat the oven
      2. Mix the ingredients
      3. Bake for 30 minutes
      ``` The only difference is the outer tag: `
        ` for bullets, `
          ` for numbers. The individual items are always `
        1. ` (list item) regardless. A common mistake: using `
            ` when the content has a meaningful order (like steps in a process). If the sequence matters, it should be `
              `.

problems.question: What will the user see when the browser renders this HTML? ```html Visit our store ```
options: A) The text "Visit our store" displayed as a clickable link
B) The URL "https://shop.example.com" displayed on the page
C) A button labeled "Visit our store"
problems.explanation: The <a> tag creates a hyperlink. The text between the opening and closing tags — "Visit our store" — is what the user sees as clickable text. The href attribute defines where the link goes when clicked, but the URL itself is not displayed on the page.

problems.question: A product page shows a broken image icon where the product photo should be. Here is the HTML: ```html ``` You check the server and find the image file is actually located at `/images/store/widget.png`. What is the problem?
options: A) The alt text does not match the file name
B) The src path points to /images/products/ but the file is in /images/store/
C) The tag is missing a closing tag
problems.explanation: The src attribute tells the browser where to find the image file. The path says /images/products/widget.png, but the file actually lives at /images/store/widget.png. The browser cannot find the file at the specified path, so it shows a broken image icon. Changing the src to the correct path fixes it. (And tags are self-closing — they never need a closing tag.)

problems.question:
A designer reviews a page and says: "These setup steps should be numbered, not bullet points — the order matters." You look at the HTML and see: ```html
  • Create an account
  • Verify your email
  • Set up your profile
``` What would you tell AI to fix?
options: A) Change the <ul> tag to <ol> so the list renders as numbered instead of bulleted
B) Add numbers manually to each <li> text, like "1. Create an account"

C) Replace the
  • tags with

    tags to make them numbered
problems.explanation: The difference between a bulleted list and a numbered list in HTML is just the outer tag: <ul> (unordered) for bullets, <ol> (ordered) for numbers. The <li> items inside stay the same. You would tell AI: "Change the <ul> to <ol> so the steps are numbered." Adding numbers manually to the text would be redundant and fragile — if you reorder the items, you would have to renumber them by hand.

blocks.title: Go to any webpage you visit regularly — your company's site, a news article, a product page. Right-click and select "View Page Source" (or press Ctrl+U / Cmd+Option+U). Search for <a, <img, and <ul or <ol. Can you find a link and identify where it goes? Can you find an image and read its alt text?
blocks.solution: You should be able to find tags where the href shows the destination URL, and tags where src is the image path and alt is the description. If you found these and could read what they do, you are building real code literacy.

blocks.content:
Links (), images (), and lists (
    /
      ) are the most common HTML elements you will encounter. They use attributes (href, src, alt) to provide extra information beyond just labeling content. When these elements break, the fix is almost always a wrong or missing attribute value — a bad URL, a wrong file path, or missing alt text.

blocks.prompt: You have now covered the core building blocks of HTML: tags, page structure, and the most common elements. Time to put it all together. The checkpoint will test your ability to read and troubleshoot real HTML — just like you would in your actual work.

reviewQuestions.question: The HTML tag for creating a link is:
reviewQuestions.explanation: The (anchor) tag creates clickable links. The href attribute specifies where the link goes: Click here.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's it! The <a> tag (for 'anchor') creates links.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Links use the <a> (anchor) tag with an href attribute. <link> is used in the <head> for stylesheets, not for clickable links.

reviewQuestions.question: The 'alt' attribute on an tag is used for:
options: Providing a text description for screen readers and when the image fails to load
reviewQuestions.explanation: The alt attribute describes the image in text. It's essential for accessibility (screen readers) and displays when the image can't load.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right! Alt text is crucial for accessibility and as a fallback.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The alt attribute provides a text description of the image. Screen readers read it aloud, and it displays if the image fails to load.

reviewQuestions.question: When a link or image on a webpage is broken, the fix is almost always:
options: A wrong or missing attribute value (like a bad URL in href or src)
reviewQuestions.explanation: Broken links and images are almost always caused by incorrect href or src attribute values — a typo, wrong path, or missing file.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly! Check the href or src attribute first when links or images break.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: When links or images break, the cause is almost always a wrong or missing attribute value — typically a bad URL in href or src.
